turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in case 21-846, Cruz versus Arizona.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This Court held in Simmons versus South Carolina that when a capital defendant's future dangerousness is at issue and the only two sentencing options under state law are death or life imprisonment without the possibility of parole, then the defendant has a constitutional right to inform the jury that if they sentence him to life in prison, he will not be eligible for parole. For many years, capital defendants into in Arizona including petitioner, John Montenegro Crews have been unable to assert this right. That is because in case after case, the Arizona Supreme Court decided wrongly that Arizona's sentencing laws did not trigger application of Simmons. This Court put a stop to that when it summarily reversed the Arizona Supreme Court in Lynch versus Arizona, thus settling once and for all that Simmons applies in Arizona. Following this Court's Lynch decision, Cruz filed the successive petition for state post-conviction relief in Arizona, seeking once again to assert his rights under Simmons. He invoked Arizona Rule of Criminal procedure 32.1(g), which allows for successive positions if there has been a significant change in the law that if applicable to the defendant's case would probably overturn the defendant's judgment or sentence. The Arizona Supreme Court denied Cruz's position. It concluded that Lynch was not a significant change in the law for purposes of Rule 32.1(g). The question presented in this case is whether that procedural ruling is an adequate and independent state court ground for the Judgment, thus barring any further review of Cruz's claim in Arizona. We hold that the Arizona Supreme Court's decision in this case is inadequate. Typically, this Court will not take up a question of federal law in a case decided by a federal court if the state court's decision rests on a state procedural rule that is “firmly established and regularly followed.” But this Court has long held that novel state procedural requirements should not foreclose review of federal claims. Thus when state courts deny relief based on new and unforeseeable procedural requirements that lack fair or substantial support in prior state law, those rulings are not adequate to bar further review. Such was the case here. The basic problem is this. The Arizona Supreme Court has stated over and over that the archetypal type of a significant change in the law under Rule 32.1(g) is the overruling of binding precedent, and Lynch clearly overruled binding Supreme Court precedent. Yet, the Arizona Supreme Court concluded that Lynch was not a significant change in the law. It supported that unexpected conclusion by pointing out that Lynch did not change federal law even though it changed state law. But Lynch did change the law in Arizona, and whether there has been a significant change in Arizona law has always been what Arizona courts looked to when applying Rule 32.1(g). By sharply departing from that prior practice, the Arizona Supreme Court applied 32.1(g) in a completely novel and unforeseeable way that conflicted with its prior cases. Such a decision is not adequate under our law to bar federal review. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we vacate and remand for further consideration of Cruz's petition. Justice Barrett has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas, Alito, and Gorsuch have joined.
